verses.text: Then Nahash the Ammonite came up, and encamped against Jabesh-gilead: and all the men of Jabesh said unto Nahash, Make a covenant with us, and we will serve thee. And Nahash the Ammonite answered them, On this condition will I make a covenant with you, that I may thrust out all your right eyes, and lay it for a reproach upon all Israel. And the elders of Jabesh said unto him, Give us seven days' respite, that we may send messengers unto all the coasts of Israel: and then, if there be no man to save us, we will come out to thee. Guidance: Nahash ('serpent') wants more than tribute; he wants disfigurement. The right eye was the archer's aiming eye and the soldier's targeting eye against the shield held in the left hand — gouging it crippled the city's defense capability for generations. The terms are not just cruel; they are calibrated humiliation, designed to be 'a reproach upon all Israel.' The elders of Jabesh respond with strange composure: seven days' respite, with a chance to seek deliverance. Sometimes the first faith-act of a besieged people is simply to ask for one week and call out for help — and to hold their nerve while they wait.

Then came the messengers to Gibeah of Saul, and told the tidings in the ears of the people: and all the people lifted up their voices, and wept. And, behold, Saul came after the herd out of the field; and Saul said, What aileth the people that they weep? And they told him the tidings of the men of Jabesh. Guidance: The messengers reach Saul's hometown, and the whole village erupts in lament. Saul — anointed and acclaimed king two chapters ago — is in the field behind the oxen. The first kingship has not produced a palace, a standing army, or a war room; Saul is still farming. He comes in from work and asks why everyone is weeping. The picture is striking: Israel's king-in-name discovers a national crisis the way any villager would — by walking in from the field at sunset and asking. The crown is not yet a reflex; it has to be activated by an actual emergency. God will provide the activation in the next verse.

Guidance: The messengers reach Saul's hometown, and the whole village erupts in lament. Saul — anointed and acclaimed king two chapters ago — is in the field behind the oxen. The first kingship has not produced a palace, a standing army, or a war room; Saul is still farming. He comes in from work and asks why everyone is weeping. The picture is striking: Israel's king-in-name discovers a national crisis the way any villager would — by walking in from the field at sunset and asking. The crown is not yet a reflex; it has to be activated by an actual emergency. God will provide the activation in the next verse.

And the Spirit of God came upon Saul when he heard those tidings, and his anger was kindled greatly. And he took a yoke of oxen, and hewed them in pieces, and sent them throughout all the coasts of Israel by the hands of messengers, saying, Whosoever cometh not forth after Saul and after Samuel, so shall it be done unto his oxen. And the fear of the LORD fell on the people, and they came out with one consent. Guidance: Three things land together: 'the Spirit of God came upon Saul' (the same Spirit who came at the prophets' band, now turned to warlike action); 'his anger was kindled' (righteous wrath at injustice — the engine of just war); and a violent symbolic act (chopped oxen sent through the twelve tribes, echoing the Levite's grim summons in Judges 19). The yoke of oxen — his own working animals — becomes the call-up notice. Cost goes first: the man asking Israel to risk her livestock and lives sacrifices his own oxen at the start. 'The fear of the LORD fell on the people, and they came out with one consent' — for one shining moment, the fragmented tribes act as one nation under God. Spirit-filled leadership, righteous anger, and personal sacrifice are still the recipe that gathers a divided people.

And when he numbered them in Bezek, the children of Israel were three hundred thousand, and the men of Judah thirty thousand. And they said unto the messengers that came, Thus shall ye say unto the men of Jabesh-gilead, Tomorrow, by that time the sun be hot, ye shall have help. And the messengers came and shewed it to the men of Jabesh; and they were glad. The men of Jabesh therefore said, Tomorrow we will come out unto you, and ye shall do with us all that seemeth good unto you. Guidance: Three hundred thirty thousand muster at Bezek — already a tenfold-larger force than the four thousand Israel could field at Aphek in chapter 4. Note the early distinction between 'children of Israel' and 'men of Judah' — the tribal split that will become the divided kingdom is already a categorical seam in the army roster, though they fight under one banner here. The promise is precise: 'tomorrow, by the time the sun be hot.' Faith does not announce a vague 'someday'; it gives the besieged a deadline they can count on. Jabesh's response is genius — they tell Nahash they will surrender 'tomorrow,' and 'do with us all that seemeth good' — true, but the 'you' will be Saul's army, not Nahash's. Sometimes deliverance arrives disguised as compliance.

And it was so on the morrow, that Saul put the people in three companies; and they came into the midst of the host in the morning watch, and slew the Ammonites until the heat of the day: and it came to pass, that they which remained were scattered, so that two of them were not left together. Guidance: 'Three companies' — the same tactical arrangement Gideon used against Midian, the textbook ambush of the period. The 'morning watch' is the last shift of the night, roughly 2-6am, when Ammon was sleeping off their pre-dawn confidence. Saul attacks before sunrise; the slaughter lasts until 'the heat of the day' — perhaps 10am. By midday the threat is over and the survivors are so scattered 'two of them were not left together' — a vivid touch: not even pairs survived to regroup. Saul's first military act delivers exactly on the promise of v9 ('by the time the sun be hot'). The new king can lead an army. The crown begins to fit.

And the people said unto Samuel, Who is he that said, Shall Saul reign over us? bring the men, that we may put them to death. And Saul said, There shall not a man be put to death this day: for to day the LORD hath wrought salvation in Israel. Guidance: With the battlefield still steaming, the crowd remembers the 'sons of Belial' who had despised Saul at the coronation (10:27). They turn to Samuel — still the kingmaker — and ask permission to execute the dissenters. Saul intervenes: 'There shall not a man be put to death this day.' The reason is theological, not pragmatic: 'today the LORD hath wrought salvation' — a day of God's deliverance is not a day for settling personal scores. The early Saul rules with grace; he refuses to use his first victory as cover for political purges. This is the high point of his kingship; nothing he does afterward will be quite this magnanimous. Power is most reliably tested not by how it confronts enemies but by how it forgives its critics.

The early Saul rules with grace; he refuses to use his first victory as cover for political purges. This is the high point of his kingship; nothing he does afterward will be quite this magnanimous. Then said Samuel to the people, Come, and let us go to Gilgal, and renew the kingdom there. And all the people went to Gilgal; and there they made Saul king before the LORD in Gilgal; and there they sacrificed sacrifices of peace offerings before the LORD; and there Saul and all the men of Israel rejoiced greatly. Guidance: Samuel calls them all back to Gilgal — the place where Joshua first circumcised the new generation and the manna ceased (Josh 4–5) — to 'renew the kingdom.' The Mizpeh coronation was contested by some; this Gilgal renewal will be unanimous. The order is significant: military victory first, then public covenant — Saul has now demonstrated kingship in deed, so the people can confirm what God had already declared. Peace offerings ('shelamim') are the festal sacrifice where worshipers eat the meal in God's presence — communion, not just expiation. The chapter ends with three words you should circle: 'rejoiced greatly.' This is the brightest hour of Saul's reign and one of the brightest hours of the early monarchy. Hold the picture; the book will darken from here.
